title: Reading and Use of English – Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: From Bean to Bar: The Craft Chocolate Revolution
content.text: Most people think of chocolate as something that simply appears on a supermarket shelf, wrapped in foil and ready to eat. Few consider the extraordinary journey that a cacao bean makes before it becomes the smooth, glossy square that melts on the tongue. (37) [37] The craft movement has refocused attention on every stage of the journey from tree to finished bar. [/37] The craft chocolate movement has given producers, retailers and consumers a new vocabulary for talking about one of the world's most complex flavour experiences. || Everything begins in the tropics. Cacao trees grow only within about twenty degrees of the equator, in countries such as Ecuador, Ghana, Madagascar and Vietnam. The trees produce large, ribbed pods that contain seeds — the cacao beans — surrounded by a sweet white pulp. (38) [38] The quality of the bean at harvest is the single greatest determinant of the chocolate's eventual flavour. [/38] The variety of the cacao, the soil in which it grows and even the specific hillside on which it is cultivated can all influence the flavour profile of the finished product, much as terroir shapes wine. || After harvesting, the beans undergo fermentation. Workers pile them into wooden boxes or cover them with banana leaves and leave them for several days, during which microorganisms transform the raw beans chemically. (39) [39] Fermentation is the critical step that develops the flavour precursors that roasting will later bring to life. [/39] If fermentation is rushed or poorly managed, no amount of skill in later stages can fully compensate for the resulting flatness of flavour. || Once fermented and dried, the beans travel to the chocolate maker's workshop. There, they are roasted in small batches at carefully controlled temperatures — a step where the distinctive aromas we associate with chocolate are finally unlocked. (40) [40] The chocolate maker adjusts roasting time and temperature to highlight the unique characteristics of each origin. [/40] This is where the craft chocolate maker distinguishes themselves most clearly from industrial producers, who roast enormous quantities to a standard profile. || The roasted beans are then cracked, their shells removed, and the inner nibs ground into a smooth liquid called chocolate liquor. Added cocoa butter, sugar and — for milk chocolate — dairy solids transform this liquid through extended mixing and grinding, known as conching. (41) [41] Conching can last anywhere from a few hours to several days, depending on the desired texture and flavour. [/41] Longer conching generally produces a smoother, more refined texture and develops more nuanced flavours. || The final step is tempering: carefully cycling the melted chocolate through specific temperatures to encourage the formation of stable crystal structures. (42) [42] Correct tempering gives the finished chocolate its characteristic gloss and satisfying snap. [/42] It is a process that demands precision and experience, and it is the last of many steps that separate a truly outstanding bar from an ordinary one.

paragraphs.A: Fermentation is the critical step that develops the flavour precursors that roasting will later bring to life.
paragraphs.B: The craft movement has refocused attention on every stage of the journey from tree to finished bar.
paragraphs.C: The quality of the bean at harvest is the single greatest determinant of the chocolate's eventual flavour.
paragraphs.D: The chocolate maker adjusts roasting time and temperature to highlight the unique characteristics of each origin.
paragraphs.E: Conching can last anywhere from a few hours to several days, depending on the desired texture and flavour.
paragraphs.F: Correct tempering gives the finished chocolate its characteristic gloss and satisfying snap.
paragraphs.G: Some craft producers travel personally to cacao farms to select beans and observe the fermentation process.

questions.explanation: B introduces the role of the artisan movement in revaluing each stage of the process, connecting with the new perspective the article develops.
C establishes that the quality of the bean at the source determines the final flavour, preparing the comparison with the concept of wine terroir.
A explains the essential function of fermentation for flavour development, linking to the warning about poor fermentation.
D describes how the master chocolatier personalises the roasting, differentiating it from the industrial process mentioned subsequently.
E specifies the variable duration of conching, connecting with the following statement about its benefits in texture and flavour.
F describes the visible result of correct tempering (shine and snap), closing the technical description of the process.
